cited_text: And so one, to, to lever up is scary to people, but then two, to lever up to buy your own shares when everyone's telling you your company is a piece of shit, that's really scary to do.
response_text:
The quote lands because the fear was rational —
stock down 92%, yet he borrowed to buy more.
The contrarian move only works if you trust your
own fundamentals over the market's signal.

cited_text: One is the whole world doesn't like our shares. So if no one's going to buy our shares, why don't we just start buying our own shares? And so we kicked off a really successful buyback.
response_text:
Simple logic, hard to execute under pressure.
Most founders in a 92% drawdown go defensive.
Foroughi went offensive with leverage. The gap
between knowing this and doing it is enormous.

cited_text: I didn't go to VCs until knew we were going to be an advertising business.
response_text:
This is the anti-pitch-deck move. He bootstrapped
through the messy pivot phase, only raising when
there was real product-market fit. Means he never
had to defend bad early decisions to investors.

cited_text: One of the things that I always looked at for hiring were find people who have a chip on their shoulder, someone who has a reason to push hard. And I had that myself.
response_text:
This is his actual hiring filter — not credentials,
not pedigree. It explains the high school dropout
(Raf) who became a billionaire deal-maker. Hunger
is the variable most hiring processes screen out.

cited_text: I mean, mine was that my family was uprooted out of Iran. We ended up coming here when I was 4. But my dad had one of the most successful real estate development companies in the country. So he was loaded. Very successful. Back in the day, thousands people worked for him and we got booted out of our country.
response_text:
The origin of the chip. His father lost everything
to revolution, and Foroughi internalised that as
obligation, not just ambition. That's a different
quality of drive — harder to turn off.

cited_text: I didn't even know what a state owned business was at the point in time it turned out like. And we announced the deal.
response_text:
The China deal nearly destroyed the company not
through malice but through ignorance of geopolitics.
A board would have flagged CFIUS immediately.
This is the clearest case he makes for why
six years without one was a mistake.

cited_text: But it was important for me to get it to that point because to your point of having A players, A players don't like to work with B, Cs, Ds, I mean, Ds, you're gonna just burn your A's out. But like anything that's worse than an A player on the team and you're distracting your best for working with those types of people. And like, trying to bring them up to speed is a huge loss because eventually you end up churning out your A.
response_text:
The compounding cost of a mediocre hire isn't
the mediocre hire — it's the A player who leaves
because of them. Most orgs never price this in.
Foroughi did, and fired 40% of the team while
growing revenue ~100% YoY.

cited_text: So we went from somewhere in the neighborhood of hundreds of new hires that we're doing a year, or at least attempting to do a year, to in the tens because people were so spooked about trying to convince me that they need the role, they wouldn't Come to me.
response_text:
Forcing managers to justify headcount to the CEO
is blunt but effective. The friction is the feature.
If a manager won't fight for the hire, they probably
didn't need it. This is how you stop autopilot bloat.

cited_text: And because the reason I don't is that now with AI tools, one person who's really good can be as powerful as 10, 20, 50, 100 people. And so why would I go higher in advance of that versus go find those single individuals who could be really good who understand how to deploy agents to tackle jobs that many people would have done before.
response_text:
He's not making a vague AI point here — he's using
it as a **reason not to hire ahead of demand**.
Most companies treat AI as a productivity multiplier
on existing headcount. He treats it as a reason
to keep headcount flat or shrinking.

cited_text: Your 1x engineer might be 2x more efficient, but your 10x engineer might be 100x more efficient. Not only can they go out and accelerate the rate of speed that they can create experiments and improvements on machine learning models.
response_text:
The non-linearity is the key claim. AI doesn't
lift everyone equally — it amplifies judgment,
not just output. A-players know how to use it;
B-players generate more mediocre output faster.
This is worth sitting with.

cited_text: The hope we have is that we can get the local laundromat discovered by someone playing a game because we serve a really good ad to someone who needs their clothes wash. If that happens and we get to that level of scale, this business is going to be much, much bigger than it is today. And the opportunity for us to really impact economic growth for businesses that are small to medium size is going to be much bigger than it is today.
response_text:
This is the long-term bull case stated plainly.
Gaming as a distribution layer for **all** SMB
advertising — not just e-commerce. Whether the
targeting gets precise enough is the open question,
but the surface area of the opportunity is real.
